questions.stem: For each provider order, click to specify whether the nurse should anticipate it as an early DKA priority or not an early priority.
questions.scenario: The client is lethargic, hypotensive, and profoundly dehydrated with glucose 612 mg/dL and serum bicarbonate 12 mEq/L.

options.text: Early DKA priority
Not an early priority

matrixRows.label: Potassium review before continuous insulin progression
Delay treatment until ketones are redrawn

questions.rationale: DKA decisions become faster when the nurse anchors to perfusion first. Fluids and safe insulin progression with potassium awareness are early priorities; routine long-acting insulin and delays for extra labs do not solve the immediate shock/dehydration problem.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization. The stem cue "For each provider order, click to specify whether the nurse should anticipate it as an early DKA priority" makes unstable glucose physiology the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: Because dKA decisions become faster when the nurse anchors to perfusion first suggests unstable glucose physiology, "Not an early priority" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Think perfusion first in DKA, then safe insulin progression with potassium awareness.
questions.speedCue: If the stem shows hypotension, dehydration, and very high glucose, fluids belong in the early-priority column right away.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: For each newborn finding, click to specify whether the finding supports hypoglycemia concern or is less specific for hypoglycemia.
questions.scenario: The newborn was delivered by a client with gestational diabetes and has not fed well since birth.

options.text: Supports hypoglycemia concern
Less specific for hypoglycemia

questions.rationale: This item trains quick clue sorting. Jitteriness and weak feeding in a high-risk newborn support hypoglycemia concern; the other findings are less specific and should not distract the nurse from checking and treating glucose promptly.

distractorRationales.a: "Supports hypoglycemia concern" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Jitteriness and weak feeding in a high-risk newborn support hypoglycemia concern" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "the row-specific answer pattern" fits the safer response to newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.

questions.takeaway: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.
questions.speedCue: In a newborn of a diabetic parent, jitteriness is not a soft clue; it should immediately raise glucose concern.

visualRationale.title: newborn_hypoglycemia_jitteriness
visualRationale.caption: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.
visualRationale.conclusion: Newborn jitteriness paired with poor feeding is a check-glucose-now clue set.

questions.stem: For each maternal finding, click to specify whether the finding is more consistent with placental abruption or less consistent with placental abruption.
questions.scenario: The client is 35 weeks pregnant and arrives after reporting severe abdominal pain.

options.text: More consistent with placental abruption
Less consistent with placental abruption

questions.rationale: Placental abruption becomes easier to answer when the learner locks onto pain, uterine tone, and fetal compromise clues. Painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of bleeding-risk intervention. The stem cue "Placental abruption becomes easier to answer when the learner locks onto pain, uterine tone" makes bleeding or anticoagulation injury the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.b: Because painless bright-red bleeding points away from abruption and more toward placenta previa suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Less consistent with placental abruption" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Abruption is the painful, rigid, often dark-bleeding pattern.
questions.speedCue: Pain and uterine rigidity should move abruption to the top of the differential before you even finish the stem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with sodium 118 mEq/L becomes confused and then has a seizure. Place the actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: Emergency supplies are available and the provider is on the way.

options.text: Prepare to administer hypertonic saline as prescribed
Protect the airway and initiate seizure precautions
Notify the provider or rapid response team with the acute change
Reassess neurologic status after initial stabilization

questions.rationale: In symptomatic hyponatremia, the nurse starts with seizure safety and airway protection, escalates immediately, prepares for controlled hypertonic saline correction, and then reassesses neurologic response. The reasoning is faster when the learner links low sodium with acute neurologic collapse.
questions.takeaway: When low sodium causes seizure, think protect, escalate, correct, then reassess.
questions.speedCue: The key clue is not just a sodium number; it is low sodium plus acute neurologic change.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client has been actively seizing for more than 5 minutes. Place the nursing actions in the order the nurse should take them.
questions.scenario: The client is on a medical unit bed with oxygen available and IV access present.

options.text: Administer IV lorazepam per protocol
Protect the airway and turn the client to the side
Prepare longer-acting antiepileptic therapy after first-line control
Call for rapid assistance and obtain bedside support

questions.rationale: Status epilepticus is managed as an airway-plus-medication emergency. The nurse starts with immediate safety and airway support, activates help, gives a benzodiazepine quickly, and then prepares the next-line antiepileptic plan once the seizure is being controlled.
questions.takeaway: Past 5 minutes, seizure care becomes airway support plus benzo-first treatment in a clear sequence.
questions.speedCue: The time trigger is the clue: once the seizure keeps going, stop thinking watchful waiting and move to airway plus benzodiazepine.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client reports sudden pounding headaches, diaphoresis, palpitations, and a blood pressure of 236/128 mm Hg. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately and anticipate rapid blood pressure control for a catecholamine crisis.
Have the client rest quietly and recheck the blood pressure at the next routine assessment.
Encourage the client to drink fluids because dehydration is the most likely cause.
Document the episode as anxiety because the symptoms are intermittent.

questions.rationale: Paroxysmal severe hypertension with headache, diaphoresis, and palpitations is a classic clue cluster for pheochromocytoma crisis. The nurse should escalate immediately because end-organ injury, dysrhythmia, or stroke can occur if the blood pressure is not controlled. The time-saving exam clue is the triad pattern, not just the elevated number.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "a blood pressure of 236/128 mm Hg" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Notify the provider immediately and anticipate rapid blood pressure control for a catecholamine crisis" fits the safer response to paroxysmal severe hypertension with headache, diaphoresis.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the episode as anxiety because the symptoms are intermittent" misses the bedside risk because the nurse should escalate immediately because end-organ injury, dysrhythmia, or stroke can occur if the blood pressure is points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: Intermittent pounding headache, diaphoresis, and extreme hypertension should trigger pheochromocytoma crisis thinking fast.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: One hour after birth a client has a boggy fundus, saturates a perineal pad in 15 minutes, and has a pulse of 118/min. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Massage the fundus and call for assistance.
Ask the client to breastfeed before intervening.
Prepare the client for discharge teaching.
Recheck the lochia in 1 hour.

questions.rationale: A boggy fundus with heavy bleeding suggests uterine atony, the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage. The nurse should massage the fundus immediately while summoning help and preparing further interventions. Waiting or delaying treatment increases the risk of rapid hemodynamic deterioration.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave bleeding or anticoagulation injury untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Massage the fundus and call for assistance" fits the safer response to boggy fundus plus heavy lochia means treat uterine atony immediately with fundal massage and escalation.
distractorRationales.c: "Prepare the client for discharge teaching" misses the bedside risk because has a pulse of 118/min points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.d: "Recheck the lochia in 1 hour" misses the bedside risk because has a pulse of 118/min points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Boggy fundus plus heavy lochia means treat uterine atony immediately with fundal massage and escalation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client 12 hours after transsphenoidal hypophysectomy has a steady drip of clear drainage from the nose and reports a salty taste in the throat. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because the findings suggest a cerebrospinal fluid leak.
Encourage the client to cough and deep-breathe to clear the drainage.
Insert nasal packing to stop the drainage.
Place the client flat and ask about seasonal allergies.

questions.rationale: Clear nasal drainage with a salty taste after transsphenoidal surgery is a high-yield clue for CSF leak. The nurse should report it immediately and protect the repair by avoiding maneuvers that raise intracranial or nasal pressure. The exam shortcut is to link the postop route with the drainage description rather than overthinking routine sinus drainage.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Notify the provider because the findings suggest a cerebrospinal fluid leak" fits the safer response to a client 12 hours after transsphenoidal hypophysectomy has a steady drip of clear drainage from the nose.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Notify the provider because the findings suggest a cerebrospinal fluid leak" fits the safer response to the nurse should report it immediately and protect the repair by avoiding maneuvers that raise intracranial or nasal.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Notify the provider because the findings suggest a cerebrospinal fluid leak" fits the safer response to the nurse should report it immediately and protect the repair by avoiding maneuvers that raise intracranial or nasal.

questions.takeaway: Clear salty nasal drainage after transsphenoidal surgery is a report-now CSF leak pattern.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100.8 F (38.2 C), tenderness over the graft site, and decreased urine output. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because these findings may indicate acute rejection.
Reassure the client that mild fever is expected after transplant.
Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose.
Delay action until the next routine clinic visit.

questions.rationale: Fever, graft tenderness, and decreased urine output after kidney transplant are concerning for acute rejection. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and intervention. Missed or delayed response can threaten graft survival.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Instruct the client to skip the next immunosuppressant dose" uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. The cue "decreased urine output" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before teaching or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "A client 2 weeks after kidney transplant has a temperature of 100" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Graft tenderness with falling urine output after transplant is a report-now pattern.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: During labor a client suddenly becomes short of breath, cyanotic, and hypotensive. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen.
Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass.
Obtain a routine pain score before intervening.
Document the event as an anxiety reaction.

questions.rationale: Sudden hypoxia and hypotension during labor raise concern for amniotic fluid embolism, a catastrophic obstetric emergency. The nurse should escalate immediately and support oxygenation and circulation. Delay can rapidly endanger both the client and fetus.

distractorRationales.b: "Reposition the client and wait to see if the symptoms pass" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Sudden maternal collapse in labor requires immediate emergency response and oxygenation support" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: Because sudden hypoxia and hypotension during labor raise concern for amniotic fluid embolism, a catastrophic obstetric emergency suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Obtain a routine pain score before intervening" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Call for immediate emergency support and begin high-flow oxygen" fits the safer response to during labor a client suddenly becomes short of breath, cyanotic.

questions.takeaway: Sudden maternal collapse in labor requires immediate emergency response and oxygenation support.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" misses the bedside risk because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" misses the bedside risk because severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" misses the bedside risk because severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered points to neurologic deterioration. Neurologic change needs prompt assessment and safety measures before reassurance, discharge, or routine reassessment.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
